title: Show Me Districts
description: Missouri congressional redistricting transparency tool with an interactive map, collaborative district editor, and fair-map comparisons. Built for nonpartisan civic engagement.

detail.tagline: An interactive, nonpartisan look at how Missouri's congressional maps are drawn — and how fair they really are.
detail.role: Solo — data, maps, fairness metrics, and site.
overview: Missouri redrew its congressional map in a rare mid-cycle special session in 2025 — the kind of quiet, procedural change that quietly decides elections. Show Me Districts makes that process legible: an explicitly nonpartisan tool to see how the state's eight districts have been drawn over two centuries, redraw them yourself, and measure how fair any map actually is.

The fairness scores aren't hand-waving. Polsby-Popper and Reock compactness, population deviation, and the efficiency gap are computed in the browser with Turf.js from real census-tract geometry and decades of election returns. Draw a plan in the editor and every metric updates as you paint.

gallery.caption: District History — Missouri's boundaries across two centuries as a partisan-lean choropleth; scrub the timeline and click a district for its election results.
Draw Your Map — paint the state's 1,654 census tracts into eight districts and watch population balance and fairness scores update in real time.
Fair Maps — the enacted 2022 map scored on Polsby-Popper, Reock, population deviation and efficiency gap, ready to compare against alternatives.
2025 Mid-Cycle Redistricting — a documented timeline of Missouri's out-of-cycle redraw, from the special session to the signed HB 1.

features.title: District history, 1789–2025
features.detail: Every Missouri congressional map since statehood, shaded by partisan lean, with a timeline scrubber and click-through election results per district.

features.title: Draw your own map
features.detail: Paint 1,654 census tracts into eight districts with a brush or fill tool; population balance and fairness metrics recompute on every stroke.

features.title: Fairness scoring
features.detail: Polsby-Popper, Reock, population deviation and efficiency gap — the same measures courts and scholars use — computed client-side from real geometry.

features.title: Save & compare plans
features.detail: Maps persist to a database so a plan can be saved, revisited, and compared against the current enacted map.

challenges.title: Fairness math that runs in the browser
challenges.problem: Compactness and partisan-fairness scores normally come from desktop GIS tools — not something a visitor can run live on a map they're actively drawing.
challenges.outcome: Turf.js computes Polsby-Popper, Reock, population deviation and the efficiency gap client-side from tract geometry, so every metric updates in real time as you paint, with no server round-trip.

challenges.title: Painting 1,654 tracts without lag
challenges.problem: Assigning thousands of census-tract polygons to districts by click-and-drag can bog down as the map re-renders on every stroke.
challenges.outcome: MapLibre GL renders the tract layer on the GPU and the editor updates only the changed features' fill state, so the brush stays responsive across the whole state.

challenges.title: A serverless civic tool that stays free
challenges.problem: Public-interest projects can't carry a server bill, yet saving user-drawn maps and serving decades of geodata usually implies a backend.
challenges.outcome: The site runs on Cloudflare with map saves persisted to Supabase, and heavy geometry is pre-simplified with mapshaper at build time — so it hosts cheaply and loads fast without an always-on server.

stack.name: Astro + Svelte
stack.why: Static content pages with interactive Svelte islands for the map tools.

stack.name: MapLibre GL
stack.why: GPU-rendered vector maps for history, editor, and comparison views.

stack.name: Turf.js
stack.why: Client-side geospatial math behind the fairness metrics.

stack.name: Supabase
stack.why: Persists user-drawn district plans for save and recall.

stack.name: Cloudflare
stack.why: Serverless hosting for a fast, low-cost civic tool.
